Scenario.description: Find an already-running Chrome instance or launch a new one. Sets {{tabId}} for subsequent steps.

1. LISTWINDOWS target="SYSTEM" → look for Chrome entries in the list. 2. If a Chrome instance is found: note the first tab id as {{tabId}}. 3. If Chrome is NOT found: LAUNCH target="chrome" → Chrome opens with a blank tab. Capture the PAGE:id from the CDP_NEWPAGE response or LISTWINDOWS after launch. After intro, {{tabId}} is a valid tab that can receive CDP_NAVIGATE/READ/etc.

step.note: list all sessions → filter for Chrome entries → assign {{tabId}}

Scenario.description: Launch Chrome (opens a new blank tab).
Use when Chrome is definitively not running. LAUNCH opens Chrome with the default new-tab page. Follow with CDP_NEWPAGE or CDP_NAVIGATE on the returned tab id.

step.note: launches Chrome; returns tab list with at least one blank tab

Scenario.description: Open a new blank tab. Returns PAGE:id as {{tabId}}.
CDP_NEWPAGE opens a blank tab (about:newtab) via CDP Target.createTarget. Preferred over SENDKEYS {CTRL+T} for reliable PAGE:id return. Reassign {{tabId}} from the response before continuing.

step.note: opens blank tab via CDP; capture returned PAGE:id as {{tabId}}

Scenario.description: Navigate tab {{tabId}} to {{url}}.

Uses CDP_NAVIGATE — direct CDP Page.navigate call, cleaner and more reliable than the keyboard scenario ({CTRL+L}+url+{ENTER}). After CDP_NAVIGATE, the tab loads the page; allow a brief wait before READ/CDP_PAGESOURCE. The response includes the final URL (after redirects) and page title.

step.note: navigate to URL via CDP; waits for load event

Scenario.description: Read the full visible text of the page (body.innerText).

READ with no parameter returns body.innerText — all visible text content. Useful for: verifying navigation succeeded, extracting plain-text data, checking for error messages, reading search results. For structured data prefer CDP_EXECUTE to return JSON. Does NOT return HTML markup.
Read the text content or value of element {{sel}}.
Focus the input element {{sel}} and type {{text}}.

Uses SENDKEYS with "css:{{sel}}|{{text}}" — this focuses the element then types. Use for: search boxes, login fields, form inputs. To clear first: CDP_EXECUTE "document.querySelector('{{sel}}').value=''" then fill. To submit after typing: append {ENTER} to {{text}} or use scenario "submit-form".
Click the element identified by CSS selector {{sel}}.
Fill text field {{sel}} with {{text}} and submit by pressing Enter.

Combines fill-input + {ENTER} key to submit. For multi-field forms: call fill-input for each field, then click the submit button. Monitor URL or page text change after submit to detect navigation/response.

step.note: type into field and press Enter to submit

Scenario.description: Run JavaScript {{jsCode}} in the page via CDP_EXECUTE and return the result.

CDP_EXECUTE runs Runtime.evaluate(code); returns JSON-serialized result. For async operations: wrap in an IIFE: (async () => { return await ...; })() Useful for: extracting structured data, checking element states, scrolling, triggering events, reading cookies/storage, injecting UI interactions. Example: "JSON.stringify(Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('a')).map(a=>a.href))"

step.note: Runtime.evaluate in page context; returns JSON-serialized result

Scenario.description: Capture a full-page screenshot of tab {{tabId}}.

CDP_SCREENSHOT returns a base64-encoded PNG via CDP Page.captureScreenshot. Use for: visual verification, debugging, capturing form states. The result can be decoded and saved to disk via CDP_EXECUTE or external processing.
Get the DOM accessibility snapshot of the current page (CDP_PAGESOURCE).

CDP_PAGESOURCE returns the CDP accessibility / snapshot tree (Page.getAccessibilityTree). Useful for: discovering element roles and names, finding selectors, mapping out interactive elements. Result is a JSON/XML tree; interpret roles and names to identify elements. For raw HTML source: use CDP_EXECUTE with document.documentElement.outerHTML. Depth may be limited — use CDP_EXECUTE for deep inspection of specific subtrees.
Scroll to the bottom of the page (to load lazy content).
Leave the tab open. No teardown.
No-op teardown. Use when you want the user to see the page.
Close the tab that was used for automation.

step.note: CDP Target.closeTarget; close the automation tab

Scenario.description: Navigate to about:blank (clear page state without closing tab).

step.note: navigates to blank page via CDP; tab remains open
